bottleneck.description: BatchNorm consumes 43.19% of attributed time and is memory-bound (DRAM throughput 60.5%). The '_legit_no_training' suffix confirms inference mode, meaning running_mean and running_var are constants across calls. Keeping BN as a separate operator materializes intermediate activation tensors between conv and BN, causing unnecessary DRAM round-trips on feature maps up to 128x56x56. 60 kernel launches per batch (6 BN instances x 10 measure iters) can be eliminated entirely.

transformation.description: Fold BatchNorm parameters into preceding Conv2d weights and biases at model initialization. In inference mode with fixed running_mean/running_var, BN (y = (x-mean)/sqrt(var+eps)*w + b) is absorbed into conv: w_new = w_conv * (w_bn/sqrt(var+eps)), b_new = (b_conv - mean)*(w_bn/sqrt(var+eps)) + b_bn. Eliminates all 6 BN kernel launches per forward pass.
fx_steps: Call model.eval() before any graph capture to freeze running_mean/running_var
Use torch.fx.symbolic_trace(model) to obtain the FX graph
For each Conv2d+BatchNorm2d pair, call torch.nn.utils.fuse_conv_bn_eval(conv_module, bn_module)
Alternatively use torch.quantization.fuse_modules(model, [['dw_conv', 'bn', 'relu6'], ['pw_conv', 'bn', 'relu6']]) for each block
Verify numerical equivalence: torch.testing.assert_close(original(x), fused(x), atol=1e-4, rtol=1e-4)
Re-run torch.compile(fused_model, backend='inductor') to allow Inductor to re-fuse across new op boundaries
gm.graph.lint(); gm.recompile()
transformation.code_hint:
for block in model.blocks:
    block.dw_conv = torch.nn.utils.fuse_conv_bn_eval(block.dw_conv, block.dw_bn)
    block.dw_bn = torch.nn.Identity()
    block.pw_conv = torch.nn.utils.fuse_conv_bn_eval(block.pw_conv, block.pw_bn)
    block.pw_bn = torch.nn.Identity()

bottleneck.description: All pointwise 1x1 and depthwise 3x3 convolutions arrive in NCHW memory layout. cuDNN selects im2col → sgemm for NCHW inputs rather than the NHWC implicit-GEMM path. The 'Kernel2' dominant kernel (used by the 128→256 pw convs and the aggregated pw convs) is the FP32 NCHW cuDNN GEMM, which requires 238 registers/thread, capping SM occupancy to 31% on the aggregate and 12% on the 128→256 instances. Switching to channels_last causes cuDNN to select NHWC-native kernels eligible for BF16 Tensor Core dispatch.

evidence.layout_indicator: NCHW → im2col → sgemm path (Kernel2 is cuDNN NCHW FP32 GEMM)

transformation.description: Apply torch.channels_last memory format to model weights and input tensors. This causes cuDNN to select NHWC-native conv paths, eliminating im2col overhead and enabling the cuDNN NHWC implicit-GEMM kernel which is eligible for BF16 Tensor Core dispatch on Ampere. The depthwise conv2d already uses conv2d_c1_k1_nhwc_kernel (NHWC-aware), so it benefits immediately from layout consistency.
fx_steps: model = model.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last) before torch.compile
x = x.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last) for each input batch
Set torch._inductor.config.layout_optimization = True (default on recent Inductor builds) to propagate channels_last through the graph
Verify kernel selection changed: 'Kernel2' should no longer appear in ncu output; expect 'implicit_convolve_sgemm' or similar NHWC kernel
Benchmark: channels_last typically gives 10-30% speedup on conv-heavy models on Ampere

estimated_impact.latency_reduction_pct_of_total: 5-15% on conv operators (27.1% + 10.5% of budget affected)
estimated_impact.occupancy_improvement: 238 reg/thread → ~64-96 reg/thread after OPT-003; occupancy rises from 12% to ~50%+

bottleneck.description: Pointwise 1x1 convolutions at all channel widths run in FP32. On A100 (Ampere), BF16 matmul tiles are 16x16x16 minimum; the current FP32 sgemm path uses 32x128 tiles without HMMA. Casting to BF16 routes cuBLAS/cuDNN to sm80_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16 kernels which fully engage HMMA units (312 TFLOPS BF16 vs 19.5 TFLOPS FP32 on A100). Register count typically drops from 238 to 64-96 registers/thread, directly fixing the occupancy collapse seen at occ=12% for the 128→256 pw convolutions.

evidence.theoretical_speedup: Up to 16x on pure compute-bound GEMMs; actual 1.3-2x typical at this occupancy level

transformation.description: Cast model weights and input activations to torch.bfloat16. BF16 has the same exponent range as FP32 (8 bits) with reduced mantissa (7 vs 23 bits), making it numerically safe for inference without calibration. On A100 this engages HMMA Tensor Core units for all matmul/conv ops meeting minimum tile dimensions. Register count drops from 238 to ~64-96, raising occupancy from 12% to ~50%+.
fx_steps: model = model.to(torch.bfloat16) after BN folding (OPT-001) and channels_last (OPT-002)
x = x.to(torch.bfloat16) for all input tensors
Set torch.backends.cudnn.allow_tf32 = True (default on Ampere) for additional FC layer benefit
With torch.compile: pass torch.compile(model, backend='inductor', mode='max-autotune') after dtype cast; Inductor will auto-select BF16 GEMM tiles
Validate: torch.testing.assert_close(model_fp32(x_fp32).float(), model_bf16(x_bf16).float(), atol=1e-2, rtol=1e-2)

estimated_impact.latency_reduction_pct_of_total: 10-18% on pointwise conv operators (37.6% of budget affected)
estimated_impact.occupancy_improvement: regs: 238 → ~64-96 per thread; occupancy: 12% → ~50%+ on pw128to256
estimated_impact.tensor_core_improvement: tc_active: 20-39% → ~85%+ expected for NHWC BF16 GEMM path

bottleneck.description: After BN folding (OPT-001), the hardtanh (ReLU6) activation that was fused with BN becomes a standalone elementwise op. This adds a DRAM round-trip: conv writes output, hardtanh reads and writes it back. The depthwise 128x3x3 convolutions at 1.11% each (5.55% total) are borderline memory-bound (DRAM=51%, SM=55%) and will benefit from conv-epilogue fusion eliminating the intermediate tensor write/read.

evidence.note: tc=0.6% is near-zero for depthwise — no GEMM structure, TC engagement not expected

transformation.description: Enable Inductor conv-epilogue fusion to merge the depthwise conv output with the subsequent activation. After OPT-001, hardtanh is no longer fused inside BN kernels; Inductor's epilogue_fusion config causes it to emit a single fused conv+hardtanh kernel rather than separate conv + elementwise kernels.
fx_steps: After applying OPT-001 (BN folding), OPT-002 (channels_last), and OPT-003 (BF16), run torch.compile(model, backend='inductor', mode='max-autotune')
Set torch._inductor.config.epilogue_fusion = True before compile (default True in recent builds)
Inspect generated kernel names: look for triton_poi_fused_convolution_hardtanh_* combining conv+activation
If epilogue fusion does not fire: pass bias directly to F.conv2d instead of as a separate add — cuDNN fuses bias+activation in the epilogue
Benchmark with/without epilogue_fusion to confirm HBM round-trip reduction

estimated_impact.kernel_launches_eliminated: 5 (one hardtanh per depthwise block per iteration, 5 blocks x 10 iters = 50 launches eliminated)

optimizations.notes: This optimization is a downstream benefit of OPT-001. Cannot be realized without BN folding first. Impact is modest but contributes to overall memory-traffic reduction.

global_notes: All duration_ns values are ncu replay times (2-5x wall clock); use for relative comparison only
A100 SXM4-80GB (Ampere, 108 SMs): peak BF16 312 TFLOPS, HBM2e 2.0 TB/s, ridge point ~156 FLOP/byte
OPT-001 through OPT-003 are synergistic: BN folding exposes clean conv+activation graphs for Inductor, channels_last enables NHWC cuDNN paths, BF16 engages Tensor Cores on those paths
Apply in dependency order: OPT-001 → OPT-002 → OPT-003 → OPT-004
warp_cycles_per_instruction returned null for all kernels — eligible_cycles_pct used as latency-bound proxy (Ampere quirk)
